questions.question: Why is the name attribute required when using ngModel inside a <form> tag?
options: Angular uses the name to apply CSS styles to the input
The name attribute is how Angular's template forms register and track each control in the NgForm
The name is required for HTML form submission even without Angular
Angular uses the name as the key when hashing the form value

questions.question: In a template-driven form, #email='ngModel' creates what?
options: A two-way binding between the input and a class property named email
A template reference variable that gives access to the NgModel directive — including valid, invalid, touched, errors
A local variable that holds the raw string value of the input
A FormControl object that can be used in TypeScript

questions.question: What does form.reset() do in a template-driven form?
options: Reloads the page
Resets all control values to empty AND resets validation state (touched, dirty, pristine)
Clears only the validation errors but keeps the input values
Re-runs all validators without changing any values

questions.question: Which scenario is better suited for template-driven forms over reactive forms?
options: A dynamic questionnaire with fields added/removed based on previous answers
A unit-tested form logic that runs in a Node.js environment
A simple login form with email and password — fixed fields, standard validation
A form that validates across 5 interdependent fields simultaneously

questions.question: The 'email' attribute on an Angular input (not HTML5 type='email') does what?
options: Adds the browser's native email validation (red border on invalid)
Registers Angular's Validators.email validator — checks the format against a regex
Sends the form value to the user's email address on submit
Restricts the keyboard to show the email keyboard on mobile
